You are a GDPR compliance analyst. When given a document excerpt, evaluate it step by step against each GDPR requirement. For each step, quote the relevant text if found, or state it is missing. End with a structured verdict listing all violations. Always follow this exact format:

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): ...
Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): ...
Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): ...
Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): ...
Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): ...
Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): ...
Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): ...
Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): ...
Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): ...
Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): ...

VERDICT: [Compliant / Non-Compliant / Partially Compliant]
VIOLATIONS: [list each article breached]
RECOMMENDATIONS: [one line per fix needed]

Assess the following privacy policy excerpt for GDPR compliance:

"We collect your name, email address, and browsing history to improve our services. Your data may be shared with our marketing partners. You can contact us at privacy@example.com to request deletion of your data."

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): [FAIL] No lawful basis stated. The document does not specify consent, legitimate interest, or any other Art. 6 basis for processing.

Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): [PASS] Not applicable. No special category data (health, biometric, etc.) appears to be collected.

Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): [PARTIAL] "To improve our services" is vague and does not constitute a specific, explicit purpose. Sharing with marketing partners implies a secondary purpose not clearly disclosed upfront.

Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): [PARTIAL] Collecting browsing history "to improve services" is not clearly justified. This may exceed what is necessary.

Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): [FAIL] Missing. No retention period or criteria for determining retention is mentioned anywhere.

Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): [PARTIAL] Only the right to erasure (Art. 17) is mentioned. Rights to access (Art. 15), portability (Art. 20), rectification (Art. 16), and objection (Art. 21) are absent.

Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): [FAIL] "Marketing partners" is named as a recipient category but no details are given — no identity, no purpose, no data processing agreement reference.

Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): [FAIL] Not addressed. It is unknown whether marketing partners are outside the EEA, and no safeguards are mentioned.

Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): [FAIL] No DPO contact or mention. Whether one is required depends on scale, but none is disclosed.

Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): [FAIL] No breach notification procedure mentioned.

VERDICT: Non-Compliant
VIOLATIONS: Art. 6 (no lawful basis), Art. 5 (vague purpose, no retention period), Art. 15-21 (incomplete rights), Art. 28 (inadequate processor disclosure), Art. 46 (no transfer safeguards)
RECOMMENDATIONS:
- State a specific lawful basis for each processing activity
- Define a clear, specific purpose for data collection
- Add a retention schedule or criteria
- List all data subject rights with instructions for exercising each
- Identify third-party processors by name or category with DPA references
- Address international transfer safeguards or confirm all processing is EEA-based
- Disclose DPO contact details if applicable

Assess the following data processing agreement excerpt for GDPR compliance:

"This Data Processing Agreement is entered into between DataCorp (Processor) and ClientCo (Controller). DataCorp shall process personal data only on documented instructions from ClientCo. Processing is limited to name, email, and purchase history for order fulfillment purposes only. Data shall be deleted within 90 days of contract termination. DataCorp shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures including encryption and access controls. DataCorp shall notify ClientCo of any personal data breach within 48 hours of becoming aware. Sub-processors may be engaged with prior written consent of ClientCo. All processing occurs within the European Economic Area."

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): [PASS] Implied. The DPA references a controller-processor relationship with documented instructions. Lawful basis determination rests with ClientCo as controller; the DPA correctly does not override this.

Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): [PASS] Not applicable. Data types listed (name, email, purchase history) do not include special category data.

Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): [PASS] Clearly stated. "Order fulfillment purposes only" is a specific, explicit purpose. Processing is restricted to controller instructions.

Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): [PASS] Reasonable. Name, email, and purchase history are proportionate to order fulfillment. No excessive data collection indicated.

Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): [PASS] Stated. "Deleted within 90 days of contract termination" provides a clear retention endpoint.

Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): [PARTIAL] Not explicitly addressed in this excerpt. A DPA should include provisions for the processor to assist the controller in responding to data subject requests. This may exist elsewhere in the full agreement.

Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): [PASS] Addressed. Sub-processors require prior written consent from ClientCo, satisfying Art. 28(2).

Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): [PASS] Compliant. "All processing occurs within the European Economic Area" eliminates transfer concerns.

Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): [PARTIAL] Not mentioned in this excerpt. Whether DataCorp requires a DPO depends on processing scale; this should be confirmed in the full agreement.

Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): [PARTIAL] "48 hours" notification to ClientCo is good practice but Art. 33 requires notification to the supervisory authority within 72 hours. The DPA should clarify roles in supervisory authority notification.

VERDICT: Partially Compliant
VIOLATIONS: Art. 28 (data subject rights assistance not confirmed), Art. 33 (supervisory authority notification role unclear)
RECOMMENDATIONS:
- Add a clause obligating the processor to assist the controller in handling data subject rights requests
- Clarify the breach notification chain — specify who notifies the supervisory authority and within what timeframe
- Confirm DPO status for DataCorp in the full agreement

Assess the following cookie consent banner text for GDPR compliance:

"We use cookies to enhance your experience. By continuing to use this site, you agree to our use of cookies."

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): [FAIL] Invalid consent. "By continuing to use this site" is not a freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous indication of agreement under Art. 7. Pre-ticked or implied consent does not satisfy GDPR.

Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): [PASS] Not applicable on its face, though behavioural tracking cookies can infer health or other special category data — this risk is not addressed.

Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): [FAIL] Vague. "Enhance your experience" is not a specific purpose. The types of cookies (analytics, marketing, functional) and their individual purposes are not disclosed.

Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): [FAIL] Cannot be assessed. Without specifying which cookies are used and what data they collect, minimization cannot be evaluated.

Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): [FAIL] Missing. No cookie duration or data retention period is stated.

Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): [FAIL] Missing. No mechanism to withdraw consent, access data, or object to processing is provided. The right to withdraw consent must be as easy as giving it (Art. 7(3)).

Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): [FAIL] Missing. No mention of third-party cookies (e.g., analytics providers, ad networks) or processors.

Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): [FAIL] Not addressed. Many cookie providers transfer data outside the EEA — this is not disclosed.

Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): [PARTIAL] Not applicable at banner level, but DPO contact should be accessible from the banner via a privacy policy link.

Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): [PASS] Not applicable at banner level.

VERDICT: Non-Compliant
VIOLATIONS: Art. 7 (invalid consent mechanism), Art. 5 (no specific purpose, no retention period), Art. 15-21 (no withdrawal or rights mechanism), Art. 28 (no third-party disclosure), Art. 46 (no transfer disclosure)
RECOMMENDATIONS:
- Replace implied consent with an explicit opt-in mechanism (e.g., Accept / Reject buttons with no pre-selection)
- Categorise cookies by type (necessary, analytics, marketing) with individual toggle controls
- State retention period for each cookie category
- Provide a clear link to withdraw consent at any time
- Disclose all third-party cookie providers and link to their privacy policies
- Address international data transfers and applicable safeguards

Assess the following employee monitoring policy for GDPR compliance:

"Employees should be aware that company devices including laptops and phones are subject to monitoring. We may review emails, internet usage, and application activity. This is necessary for security and productivity purposes. Monitoring data is retained for 12 months. Employees may submit a written request to HR to access records held about them."

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): [PARTIAL] "Security and productivity" suggests legitimate interest (Art. 6(1)(f)), but a legitimate interest assessment (LIA) is not referenced. Employee monitoring requires demonstrating the interest is not overridden by employee rights — this is not shown.

Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): [PARTIAL] Email and application monitoring could inadvertently capture health, religious, or union data. No safeguards for special category data are mentioned.

Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): [PARTIAL] Two purposes stated (security and productivity) but not separately defined. These are distinct purposes requiring separate justification; bundling them is insufficient.

Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): [FAIL] Potentially excessive. Monitoring all emails and all application activity is broad. No mention of targeted or proportionate monitoring.

Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): [PASS] "12 months" is a defined retention period.

Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): [PARTIAL] Right to access is mentioned via written HR request. Rights to rectification (Art. 16), erasure (Art. 17), restriction (Art. 18), and objection (Art. 21) are absent.

Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): [FAIL] Not addressed. If third-party security tools or cloud platforms process monitoring data, these must be disclosed.

Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): [FAIL] Not addressed. Security and monitoring tools often involve non-EEA cloud providers — not disclosed.

Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): [FAIL] Not mentioned. Large-scale systematic employee monitoring likely triggers mandatory DPO appointment under Art. 37(1)(b).

Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): [FAIL] Not addressed in this policy.

VERDICT: Non-Compliant
VIOLATIONS: Art. 6 (LIA not documented), Art. 9 (special category risk unmitigated), Art. 5 (purposes not separated, minimization concern), Art. 15-21 (incomplete rights), Art. 28 (processor disclosure missing), Art. 37 (DPO likely required)
RECOMMENDATIONS:
- Conduct and document a Legitimate Interest Assessment for each monitoring purpose
- Separate security monitoring and productivity monitoring as distinct processing activities
- Define scope limits — specify what triggers monitoring rather than blanket surveillance
- Add safeguards for incidental capture of special category data
- List all employee rights with clear exercise instructions
- Disclose third-party monitoring tool providers
- Assess DPO requirement and appoint if applicable

Assess the following healthcare app privacy notice for GDPR compliance:

"HealthTrack collects your name, date of birth, symptoms, diagnoses, and medication history to provide personalised health recommendations. We obtain your explicit consent before collecting any health information. You may withdraw consent at any time by deleting your account. Data is stored on servers in Frankfurt, Germany. We retain your health data for 5 years to enable longitudinal health tracking. You have the right to access, correct, or delete your data by contacting support@healthtrack.com. Our Data Protection Officer can be reached at dpo@healthtrack.com. In the event of a data breach, we will notify the relevant supervisory authority within 72 hours and affected users within 7 days."

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): [PASS] Explicit consent stated. "We obtain your explicit consent before collecting any health information" satisfies Art. 6(1)(a).

Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): [PASS] Health data (symptoms, diagnoses, medication history) is correctly identified as special category data, and explicit consent (Art. 9(2)(a)) is obtained prior to collection.

Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): [PASS] Clearly stated. "Personalised health recommendations" is a specific, explicit purpose. Longitudinal tracking is also disclosed as a secondary purpose with its own justification.

Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): [PASS] Reasonable. Name, DOB, symptoms, diagnoses, and medications are proportionate to the stated purpose.

Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): [PASS] "5 years" is a defined retention period with a stated rationale (longitudinal tracking).

Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): [PARTIAL] Rights to access (Art. 15), rectification (Art. 16), and erasure (Art. 17) are mentioned. Rights to portability (Art. 20), restriction (Art. 18), and objection (Art. 21) are absent. Withdrawal of consent is addressed via account deletion, but this conflates two separate actions.

Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): [FAIL] Not addressed. No mention of whether any processors handle the data.

Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): [PASS] "Servers in Frankfurt, Germany" confirms EEA-based storage. No international transfer concerns.

Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): [PASS] DPO disclosed. Contact provided at dpo@healthtrack.com.

Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): [PASS] 72-hour supervisory authority notification and 7-day user notification both stated.

VERDICT: Partially Compliant
VIOLATIONS: Art. 15-21 (incomplete rights — portability, restriction, objection missing), Art. 28 (no processor disclosure)
RECOMMENDATIONS:
- Add data portability right (Art. 20) with a mechanism to export data in machine-readable format
- Add rights to restriction (Art. 18) and objection (Art. 21) with contact instructions
- Separate consent withdrawal from account deletion
- Disclose any third-party processors and confirm DPAs are in place

Assess the following e-commerce checkout data collection notice for GDPR compliance:

"To complete your purchase, we collect your name, delivery address, and payment details. We also collect your email to send you order updates and, with your permission, marketing offers. You can unsubscribe from marketing emails at any time using the link in each email. Payment data is handled by Stripe, our PCI-compliant payment processor, and is not stored on our servers. Order data is kept for 7 years for accounting purposes. For questions about your data, email us at data@shopexample.com."

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): [PASS] Dual basis correctly applied. Purchase data processing is based on contract performance (Art. 6(1)(b)). Marketing emails require separate consent, which is correctly sought ("with your permission").

Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): [PASS] Not applicable. No special category data collected.

Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): [PASS] Purposes clearly separated. Order fulfillment, transactional emails, marketing, and accounting retention are each stated as distinct purposes.

Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): [PASS] Proportionate. Name, address, payment details, and email are standard and necessary for e-commerce transactions.

Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): [PARTIAL] "7 years for accounting purposes" covers order data. Retention of email addresses for marketing is not addressed.

Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): [PARTIAL] Unsubscribe from marketing (Art. 21 objection) is addressed. Rights to access, rectification, erasure, portability, and restriction are not mentioned.

Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): [PASS] Stripe is named as a payment processor with a relevant descriptor. Named disclosure satisfies minimum Art. 28 transparency requirements.

Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): [FAIL] Not addressed. Stripe processes data in the US — applicable transfer safeguards are not mentioned.

Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): [PARTIAL] Not addressed. Depending on scale of processing, a DPO may not be required, but this should be confirmed.

Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): [FAIL] Not addressed.

VERDICT: Partially Compliant
VIOLATIONS: Art. 5 (no marketing email retention period), Art. 15-21 (most rights not mentioned), Art. 46 (Stripe international transfer not addressed)
RECOMMENDATIONS:
- Add a retention period for marketing email addresses
- Add a full data subject rights section covering access, rectification, erasure, portability, and restriction
- Address Stripe's US data transfer — reference Standard Contractual Clauses or Stripe's DPA
- Confirm DPO requirement based on processing volume and appoint if necessary
- Add a brief breach notification commitment

Assess the following recruitment privacy notice for GDPR compliance:

"When you apply for a job at TalentCo, we collect your CV, cover letter, and contact details. We process this information to evaluate your suitability for the role. If unsuccessful, we may retain your details for up to 6 months to consider you for future roles, unless you ask us to delete them sooner. We do not share your data with third parties. You can request a copy of your data or ask us to delete it by emailing hr@talentco.com."

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): [PARTIAL] Implied but not stated. Processing for recruitment evaluation implies Art. 6(1)(b) (steps prior to contract) or Art. 6(1)(f) (legitimate interest for talent pooling). Neither is explicitly named.

Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): [PARTIAL] Not addressed. CVs frequently contain special category data (disabilities, health conditions, nationality implying ethnicity). No policy for handling or avoiding such data is stated.

Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): [PASS] Two purposes stated and separated: evaluating the current application and considering for future roles. The second purpose is opt-out rather than opt-in, which is acceptable under legitimate interest but should be clearly framed.

Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): [PASS] CV, cover letter, and contact details are standard and proportionate for recruitment.

Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): [PASS] "Up to 6 months" for unsuccessful candidates with an early deletion option is reasonable and transparent.

Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): [PARTIAL] Rights to access (Art. 15) and erasure (Art. 17) are mentioned. Rights to rectification (Art. 16), portability (Art. 20), restriction (Art. 18), and objection (Art. 21) are absent.

Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): [PASS] "We do not share your data with third parties" is a clear and compliant statement. Note: ATS software providers technically qualify as processors — if used, they should be disclosed.

Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): [PASS] Not raised as an issue given no third-party sharing stated.

Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): [FAIL] Not mentioned. An HR contact is provided but no DPO disclosed.

Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): [FAIL] Not addressed.

VERDICT: Partially Compliant
VIOLATIONS: Art. 6 (lawful basis not explicitly named), Art. 9 (special category data risk in CVs not addressed), Art. 15-21 (incomplete rights)
RECOMMENDATIONS:
- Explicitly name the lawful basis for each processing purpose
- Add a note advising applicants not to include special category data, and state how it will be handled if received
- Add rectification, portability, restriction, and objection rights
- Disclose any ATS or HR software providers as processors
- Confirm DPO requirement and disclose contact if applicable

Assess the following newsletter signup form disclaimer for GDPR compliance:

"Subscribe to our weekly newsletter. Enter your email below. By subscribing, you agree to receive marketing emails from us. We use Mailchimp to send our emails. Unsubscribe anytime."

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): [PARTIAL] Consent implied but not clearly structured. "By subscribing, you agree" suggests consent (Art. 6(1)(a)), but the mechanism must be an affirmative act. The current wording bundles signup and consent, which may not be sufficiently granular.

Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): [PASS] Not applicable. Email address only.

Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): [PARTIAL] "Marketing emails" is stated, but "weekly newsletter" and broader "marketing" are not clearly distinguished — these may involve different content types.

Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): [PASS] Email address only is proportionate for newsletter delivery.

Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): [FAIL] Missing. No retention period stated — how long is the email kept after unsubscribe?

Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): [PARTIAL] Only the right to withdraw consent (unsubscribe) is addressed. Access, erasure, portability, rectification, and restriction rights are absent.

Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): [PASS] Mailchimp is named as the email processor.

Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): [FAIL] Not addressed. Mailchimp is a US company — applicable transfer safeguards are not mentioned.

Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): [PARTIAL] Not applicable at form level, but a link to the full privacy policy should be provided.

Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): [PASS] Not applicable at form level.

VERDICT: Partially Compliant
VIOLATIONS: Art. 5 (no retention period post-unsubscribe), Art. 15-21 (only unsubscribe right mentioned), Art. 46 (Mailchimp US transfer not addressed)
RECOMMENDATIONS:
- Add a retention period — e.g., email deleted within 30 days of unsubscribe
- Link to a full privacy policy covering all data subject rights
- Address Mailchimp's US data transfer — reference their Standard Contractual Clauses
- Ensure the consent mechanism is a clear affirmative act separate from the signup action

Assess the following CCTV surveillance policy for GDPR compliance:

"CCTV cameras are in operation on these premises for security purposes. Recording is continuous 24/7. Footage is retained for 30 days and then overwritten. Access to footage is restricted to security personnel and management. Footage may be shared with law enforcement on request."

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): [PARTIAL] Security purpose implies legitimate interest (Art. 6(1)(f)), but no explicit lawful basis is stated and no legitimate interest assessment is referenced.

Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): [PARTIAL] CCTV footage can capture biometric data and reveal racial or ethnic origin. No acknowledgement or safeguard for incidental special category data capture is included.

Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): [PARTIAL] "Security purposes" is stated but vague. Sharing with law enforcement introduces a secondary purpose not separately justified.

Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): [PARTIAL] Continuous 24/7 recording may be disproportionate. No explanation of why blanket recording is necessary.

Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): [PASS] "30 days and then overwritten" is a clearly defined and reasonable retention period.

Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): [FAIL] No mention of data subject rights. Individuals captured on CCTV have the right to access footage of themselves (Art. 15) and to object (Art. 21).

Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): [PARTIAL] Law enforcement sharing is mentioned but no managed security or CCTV monitoring provider processing footage as a data processor is mentioned.

Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): [FAIL] Not addressed. If cloud storage or remote monitoring services are used, transfer implications are unaddressed.

Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): [FAIL] Not mentioned. Large-scale systematic monitoring may require a DPO and a DPIA under Art. 35.

Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): [FAIL] Not addressed.

VERDICT: Non-Compliant
VIOLATIONS: Art. 6 (lawful basis not stated), Art. 9 (biometric/special category risk unaddressed), Art. 5 (vague purpose, proportionality not justified), Art. 15-21 (no data subject rights), Art. 37 (DPO and DPIA not addressed), Art. 33 (no breach notification)
RECOMMENDATIONS:
- State legitimate interest as lawful basis and reference a documented LIA
- Acknowledge risk of capturing special category data and state safeguards
- Clarify scope of monitoring and justify continuous recording as proportionate
- Add data subject rights section including how individuals can request access to footage
- Disclose any third-party CCTV management processors
- Conduct and reference a DPIA given systematic monitoring
- Add breach notification commitment

"This Data Processing Addendum (DPA) governs processing of Customer Personal Data by Vendor. Vendor processes data solely on Customer's written instructions. Processing activities, data types, and data subject categories are set out in Annex A. Vendor shall implement technical and organisational measures as described in Annex B. Vendor shall not engage sub-processors without prior written approval from Customer. Vendor shall notify Customer of a personal data breach within 24 hours of becoming aware.
